title: Singly Linked Lists
description: A linked list is a linear data structure, in which the elements are not stored at contiguous memory locations. Instead, the elements are linked using pointers.

sections.title: Nodes and Pointers
sections.content: In a linked list, each element is an independent object called a 'Node'. A Node contains two items: the data itself, and a reference (or pointer) to the next node in the sequence. The first node is called the Head. If the list is empty, the Head points to null.

sections.title: Dynamic Size & Traversal
sections.content: Because memory does not need to be contiguous, Linked Lists can easily grow or shrink dynamically. You do not need to shift elements when inserting or deleting. However, because memory is scattered, you lose 'Random Access'. To find the 5th element, you must traverse through the 1st, 2nd, 3rd, and 4th elements sequentially.

sections.title: Real-World Application
sections.content: Linked Lists are crucial when you need constant-time insertions/deletions from a known position. They are used to implement Stacks and Queues, manage browser history (Previous/Next), and handle collision resolution in Hash Maps (Chaining).
